EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of heavy snow. ||A powerful winter storm produced multiple bands of widespread significant snowfall to parts of Western Wisconsin. A Winter Storm Watch was issued on February 19th for the entire County Warning Area to bring attention to the significant system coming a few days later. On February 21st, all counties in Western Wisconsin were upgraded to a Winter Storm Warning for wind gusts of 35-40 mph, and heavy accumulating snow of up to 12-18 inches in some areas. Using the Winter Storm Severity Index, considerable disruptions to daily life were expected. This included avoidance of travel recommendations, and potentially widespread closures to roads and other infrastructure.||Snow began to fall on the afternoon of February 21st and persisted in waves through the afternoon of February 23rd. Wind gust observations did detail some gusts reached  the low to mid 30 mph range. The first band of snow was less significant as compared to the next bands of snow. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers, verified the forecast. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8-16 inches across the region. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals included:||15.5 inches 13 miles south of Eau Claire WI.|15 inches near Hudson WI.|15.9 inches 9 miles northeast of New Richmond, WI.|14.7 inches near Chippewa Falls WI.|14.0 inches near Chetek WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast to 20 mph with gusts near 30 mph. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 4-6 inches fell in 24 hrs, additional 6-12 inches in 48 hrs and overall snow totals ranging from 8-18 inches. Per CoCoRaHS reports, there was uniform distribution of recorded snowfall ranging from 10-13 inches across the County. The highest snowfall observation was 13.4 inches located just southeast of Menomonie, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged hot and humid conditions coupled with dry mid-levels in the atmosphere, led to extreme amounts of mixed-layer convective available potential energy (MLCAPE). The existence of deep layer shear allowed for numerous strong updrafts to develop across parts of Western WI with the primary hazards being large hail and damaging winds in the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Hail measurements ranged between nickel (0.88 inch) to tea-cup (3.0 inch) size. The largest measured hail was reported 2.5 miles north of Bloomer or 5 miles NW of Chippewa Falls, WI. ||Strong winds uprooted trees, broke large tree branches across, and brought down powerlines across much of the region affected by these storms. The areas with the most wind damage occurred along a line from Polk to Rusk Counties. Strongest wind gusts were estimated to be at least 50 knots or 57 mph.
Isolated strong thunderstorms developed in the Milwaukee Metropolitan area along or near a lake breeze front or surface trough. Some large hail was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with diameter of 0.50 TO 0.75 inches.

A Winter Storm Watch was issued on February 19th for the entire County Warning Area to bring attention to the significant system coming a few days later. On February 21st, all counties in Western Wisconsin were upgraded to a Winter Storm Warning for wind gusts of 35-40 mph, and heavy accumulating snow of up to 12-18 inches in some areas. Using the Winter Storm Severity Index, considerable disruptions to daily life were expected. This included avoidance of travel recommendations, and potentially widespread closures to roads and other infrastructure.||Snow began to fall on the afternoon of February 21st and persisted in waves through the afternoon of February 23rd. Wind gust observations did detail some gusts reached  the low to mid 30 mph range. The first band of snow was less significant as compared to the next bands of snow. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers, verified the forecast. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8-16 inches across the region. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals included:||15.5 inches 13 miles south of Eau Claire WI.|15 inches near Hudson WI.|15.9 inches 9 miles northeast of New Richmond, WI.|14.7 inches near Chippewa Falls WI.|14.0 inches near Chetek WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast to 20 mph with gusts near 30 mph. Trained Spotters reported at least 12.5 inches of snowfall near Chetek, WI the morning of 2/23. However, snow continued through most of the afternoon that on 2/23 and was likely to have accumulated another inch if not more before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that developed during the summer continued into the fall. Through the 24th, Extreme Drought (D3) continued across much of Oneida, northeast Lincoln and northwest Langlade counties. Severe Drought (D2) to Moderate Drought (D1) was reported over the rest of the area except across northeast Vilas County where Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions prevailed. Much needed and sometimes heavy rainfall was recorded from the 24th through the 27th. On the October 31st U.S. Monitor, Extreme Drought (D3) was no longer reported across north-central Wisconsin. Locations that were in the Extreme Drought (D3) throughout much of the month were now classified as being in Severe Drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that developed during the summer continued into the fall. Through the 23rd, there was not much change in the drought conditions as rainfall amounts continued to run below normal, generally from an inch to an inch and a half. During this time frame, Extreme Drought (D3) persisted along the Oneida County line and across the northeast quarter of the county. Severe Drought (D2) continued over the remainder of the county, except along the Marathon County line where Moderate Drought (D1) conditions prevailed. Showers and thunderstorms brought much needed rainfall to the area, generally in the 1 to 3 inch range including Merrill 2.13 inches and Rice Reservoir 1.79 inches. Rainfall totals for the month include Merrill 4.53 inches (144% of normal) and Rice Reservoir 4.26 inches (144% of normal). On the October 31st U.S. Drought Monitor, there was no longer Extreme Drought (D3) over northeast Lincoln County; however Severe Drought (D2) remained. Moderate Drought (D1) lingered over the remainder of the county except for Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions along the Marathon County line.

Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 10 inches across central, north-central, and northeast Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 10.5 inches in Lake Tomahawk (Oneida Co.), 10.3 inches near Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), 9.4 inches in St. Germain (Vilas Co.), 8.5 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.) and 7.1 inches in Irma (Lincoln Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total across northern Oconto County was 6.5 inches in Mountain.

A woman died in a traffic accident around 7:40 AM CDT on highway 12 northeast of Fort Atkinson ,when her car crossed into the oncoming lane and struck a truck. Law enforcement determined a shallow covering of snow on the road  was a factor in the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and approaching cold front resulted in a period of snow showers across southern WI from the late morning into the afternoon. The snow showers produced a brief period of reduced visibility and accumulating snow on the roads. Two large vehicle pile-ups occurred on both sides of I-39/90 on the south side of Janesville during the early afternoon with approximately 50 injured persons. A six vehicle accident also occurred on I-94 near the IL state line in Kenosha County with no injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of snow showers with reduced visibility and accumulating snow on roads resulted in a six vehicle accident on northbound I-94 near the IL state line. No injuries were reported but the northbound lanes were closed for some time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and approaching cold front resulted in a period of snow showers across southern WI from the late morning into the afternoon. The snow showers produced a brief period of reduced visibility and accumulating snow on the roads. Two large vehicle pile-ups occurred on both sides of I-39/90 on the south side of Janesville during the early afternoon with approximately 50 injured persons. A six vehicle accident also occurred on I-94 near the IL state line in Kenosha County with no injuries reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of snow showers with reduced visibility and accumulating snow resulted in two large vehicle pile-ups on both sides of I-39/90 near Beloit and Janesville. Approximately 85 vehicles were involved in the pile-ups and around 50 people were injured and treated in area hospitals. There were a few serious injuries. Both lanes of the interstate were closed for 10 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that developed during the summer continued into the fall. Through the 24th, Extreme Drought (D3) continued across much of Oneida, northeast Lincoln and northwest Langlade counties. Severe Drought (D2) to Moderate Drought (D1) was reported over the rest of the area except across northeast Vilas County where Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions prevailed. Much needed and sometimes heavy rainfall was recorded from the 24th through the 27th. On the October 31st U.S. Monitor, Extreme Drought (D3) was no longer reported across north-central Wisconsin. Locations that were in the Extreme Drought (D3) throughout much of the month were now classified as being in Severe Drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that developed during the summer continued into the fall. From October 1st through the 23rd, there was not much change in the drought conditions as rainfall amounts continued to run below normal, generally from an inch to an inch and a half. During this time frame, the worst drought conditions existed across the northwest quarter of the county where it was reported as Extreme Drought (D3). Severe Drought (D2) extended over the remainder of the northwest half of the county while Moderate Drought (D1) persisted over the remainder of the county. Showers and thunderstorms brought much needed and sometimes heavy  rainfall to the area, generally in the 1 to 3 inch range including Antigo with 2.79 inches and Summit Lake 2.19 inches. Rainfall totals for the month include Antigo 4.90 inches (161% of normal) and Summit Lake 4.32 inches (133% of normal). On the October 31st U.S. Drought Monitor, there was no longer Extreme Drought (D3) over the far northwest part of the county. The Drought Monitor indicated Severe Drought (D2) over the far northwest portion of the county with Moderate Drought (D1) over the remainder of the northwest half of the county. The remainder of the county was classified as Abnormally Dry (D0).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system brought some freezing rain to portions of western Wisconsin from January 2nd into the 3rd. Ice accumulations around a quarter of an inch were reported south of Ettrick (Trempealeau County). The ice quickly melted on the 3rd as temperatures rose above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported around a quarter inch of ice accumulation across portions of Trempealeau County from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved into the western Great Lakes region on the 3rd and then lingered there through the 5th before weakening and moving to the east. This brought an area of snowfall to much of northwest Wisconsin starting the afternoon of the 3rd and persisting through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall totals were in the 6 to 12 inch range with higher amounts to 13 inches in Douglas County. Freezing rain was also observed at the start of the storm that led to some minor icing before changing over to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 9 to 12 inches were recorded across the county with a high report of 13 inches near Solon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of heavy snow. ||A powerful winter storm produced multiple bands of widespread significant snowfall to parts of Western Wisconsin. A Winter Storm Watch was issued on February 19th for the entire County Warning Area to bring attention to the significant system coming a few days later. On February 21st, all counties in Western Wisconsin were upgraded to a Winter Storm Warning for wind gusts of 35-40 mph, and heavy accumulating snow of up to 12-18 inches in some areas. Using the Winter Storm Severity Index, considerable disruptions to daily life were expected. This included avoidance of travel recommendations, and potentially widespread closures to roads and other infrastructure.||Snow began to fall on the afternoon of February 21st and persisted in waves through the afternoon of February 23rd. Wind gust observations did detail some gusts reached  the low to mid 30 mph range. The first band of snow was less significant as compared to the next bands of snow. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers, verified the forecast. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8-16 inches across the region. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals included:||15.5 inches 13 miles south of Eau Claire WI.|15 inches near Hudson WI.|15.9 inches 9 miles northeast of New Richmond, WI.|14.7 inches near Chippewa Falls WI.|14.0 inches near Chetek WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast to 20 mph with gusts near 30 mph. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 4-8 inches fell in 24 hrs, additional 6-8 inches in 48 hrs and overall snow totals ranging from 8-18 inches. CoCoRaHS observations ranged between 12-14 inches with the highest observation located 15 miles NNW of Wabasha, MN at 12.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure brought a cold airmass over northeast Wisconsin during the morning hours on Tuesday (1/31) with air temperatures of 5 to 25 below zero.  Light winds of 5 to 10 mph allowed wind chills to fall to 15 to 35 below zero during the morning hours on Tuesday (1/31).  The cold weather caused two fatalities in Wrightstown (Brown Co.) and Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and light winds caused wind chill values to fall to -15 to -20 during the morning hours. A teenager died of hypothermia in a field near Wrightstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of heavy snow. ||A powerful winter storm produced multiple bands of widespread significant snowfall to parts of Western Wisconsin. A Winter Storm Watch was issued on February 19th for the entire County Warning Area to bring attention to the significant system coming a few days later. On February 21st, all counties in Western Wisconsin were upgraded to a Winter Storm Warning for wind gusts of 35-40 mph, and heavy accumulating snow of up to 12-18 inches in some areas. Using the Winter Storm Severity Index, considerable disruptions to daily life were expected. This included avoidance of travel recommendations, and potentially widespread closures to roads and other infrastructure.||Snow began to fall on the afternoon of February 21st and persisted in waves through the afternoon of February 23rd. Wind gust observations did detail some gusts reached  the low to mid 30 mph range. The first band of snow was less significant as compared to the next bands of snow. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers, verified the forecast. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 8-16 inches across the region. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals included:||15.5 inches 13 miles south of Eau Claire WI.|15 inches near Hudson WI.|15.9 inches 9 miles northeast of New Richmond, WI.|14.7 inches near Chippewa Falls WI.|14.0 inches near Chetek WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast to 20 mph with gusts near 30 mph. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 3-6 inches fell in 24 hrs, additional 6-8 inches in 48 hrs and overall snow totals ranging from 8-14 inches. Trained Spotters reported 10-14 inches across the southern half of the county. The highest observed amount was near Chippewa Falls, WI with 14.5 inches.

A very brief period of freezing rain and sleet occurred in some locations with the onset of precipitation. Numerous schools were delayed over the region including a few closings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches with the highest amounts in the northwest half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that developed during the summer continued into the fall. Through the 24th, Extreme Drought (D3) continued across much of Oneida, northeast Lincoln and northwest Langlade counties. Severe Drought (D2) to Moderate Drought (D1) was reported over the rest of the area except across northeast Vilas County where Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions prevailed. Much needed and sometimes heavy rainfall was recorded from the 24th through the 27th. On the October 31st U.S. Monitor, Extreme Drought (D3) was no longer reported across north-central Wisconsin. Locations that were in the Extreme Drought (D3) throughout much of the month were now classified as being in Severe Drought (D2).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions that developed during the summer continued into the fall. Severe Drought (D2) was confined to the western third of the county throughout the month. Over the rest of the county, Moderate Drought (D1) covered the central third and the far south along the Oneida County line with Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions over the northeast quarter of the county through the 30th. Heavy rain was reported between the 24th and 27th with some location reporting 1 to 3 inches of rain. By the end of the month, Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions prevailed over the eastern half of the county while Moderate Drought (D1) was noted across the western half of the county, except for Severe Drought (D2) along the Price County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A western Wisconsin driver died in a two vehicle accident when their car lost control on State Highway 71 near Norwalk (Monroe County), crashing into an oncoming vehicle during the evening of January 4th. Light snow was reported in the area, resulting in slippery conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A driver was killed when their vehicle lost control on State Highway 71 near Norwalk during the evening of January 4th. Two other individuals involved in the crash were injured, including a passenger in the vehicle that lost control and the driver of the oncoming vehicle. Light snow was reported in the area, resulting in slippery conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain that fell during the early morning hours of January 11th resulted in icy travel conditions and multiple slide offs and crashes across much of western and central Wisconsin. Several road closures were reported that morning, including Interstate 94 between Tomah (Monroe County) and Millston (Jackson County) as well as state highways within Buffalo and Clark Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell during the early morning hours on January 11th, resulting in icy travel conditions for much of western Wisconsin. Interstate 94 was closed from Millston into Monroe County from 3:05 am to 4:00 pm due to at least 11 slide offs, 12 crashes involving property damage, and one crash with minor injuries. Interstate 94 Eastbound was also closed near Black River Falls due to automobile accidents for part of the morning.

Slippery roads along the detour on U.S. Highway 12 resulted in a jackknifed semi-trailer truck west of Warrens near Arcadia Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of 7 to 10 inches across the county, with most of it falling from the afternoon on January 03, to the morning of January 04.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record breaking snowfall for the 2022-2023 winter season was received across all of northwest Wisconsin which was about 200% of normal snowfall. Much of this snowpack remained on the ground into April. Warm temperatures 10 to 20 degrees above normal during the second week of the month led to rapid snowmelt across the region with fast rises on area waterways. This above normal warmth was quickly followed by a late season storm that brought heavy rain and heavy snow to the region and further exacerbated the ongoing flooding. Roads began being impacted on the 12th with water over roads and quickly escalated to washouts, failed culverts and gravel roads stripped over the next few days. Most of the runoff had routed through the area by the 20th with conditions improving over the remainder of the month, although localized impacts to basements, farm fields, etc. continued into early May. Due to the extent of the damage to roads, numerous entities declared states of emergency to assist with the recovery efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures early in April led to rapid snowmelt of a well above normal snowpack across the region. Additionally, heavy rainfall on top of the snowpack led to additional high runoff across the county as well. Roads began to be impacted on the 12th with water overtopping low spots leading to washouts and failed culverts. Temperatures cooled during the middle of the month along with a heavy snow event the 16th into the 17th. This allowed the runoff to slow and water levels to drop. However, water levels rose again later in the month as this new snow melted as well. Numerous road closures were reported across the entire county due to failed culverts, washouts and gravel roads being washed away. The county stated that they had run out of high water signs during the height of the event with many municipalities being out as well. Multiple communities in addition to the county issued disaster declarations due to the extent of washed out roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across the Ohio River Valley spread a swath of snow, including  blowing and drifting snow due to brisk northerly winds, across southern WI. The heaviest snow accumulated over southeast WI. Lake enhanced snow did contribute to slightly higher snow totals over far eastern WI. Small portions of the interstates over eastern WI were closed at times during the storm due to multi-vehicle accidents.  These locations were near southern Fond du Lac County, Theresa, Menomonee Falls, and Kenosha. Many schools were cancelled due to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1 to 5 inches with the highest totals in the eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system impacted Wisconsin from the evening of Saturday, April 15th into Sunday night, April 16th. Initially, rain and thunderstorms spread through southern Wisconsin before switching over to snow during the morning hours of Sunday, April 16th. Heaviest snowfall occurred in central Wisconsin during the day Sunday, April 16th, where snowfall totals were 22.5 near Longwood (Clark County) and 22.0 near Black River Falls and Northfield (Jackson County). Many trees were beginning to leaf out following record breaking warmth the week prior, and the heavy snow load resulted in tree damage and power outages. The large amount of precipitation associated with this storm greatly impacted flooding along the Mississippi, Black, and Yellow Rivers and ultimately resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration on April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer public observers reported 5 to 9 inches of snowfall across parts of Buffalo County. The highest reported total of 12 inches was recorded near Marshland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intermittent light snow from Saturday November 25th through Sunday November 26th resulted in slippery road conditions for the busy Thanksgiving holiday travel weekend. Broadcast media and law enforcement reported multiple slide offs in western and central Wisconsin. Through most of the event, snowfall rates remained under 0.1 inches per hour across western Wisconsin, increasing near 0.25 inch per hour on the evening of the 26th. The long duration resulted in a widespread 1 to 2 inches of overall accumulations by Sunday night. Highest snowfall reports were 3.3 inches near Cuba City (Grant County), and 2.0 inches near Cassville (Grant County), Thorp (Clark County), De Soto (Crawford County), and Eastman (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported 2 inches of snowfall in Thorp.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total across Florence County was 7 inches in Spread Eagle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm at the end of March provided over 1 of rainfall in central to northern Wisconsin on a deeper than average snowpack. The rapid runoff resulted in sites along the Black River in central Wisconsin to quickly rise to flood stage for the beginning of April. Minor to moderate flooding was realized at most sites with the highest height recorded at Black River Falls, with 4.17 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm at the end of March resulted in over an inch of rain falling on a deeper than average snowpack in north-central Wisconsin. The Black River at Galesville quickly rose above flood stage and crested at 13.45 ft on April 3rd, 1.45 ft above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeper than average snow pack from northern Minnesota into northern Wisconsin underwent a rapid melt through much of April from unseasonably warm temperatures combined with multiple rain events. This resulted in increased water levels and significant flooding for the entire length of the local Mississippi River, with most sites climbing to their third to fifth highest crests on record. A significant mid-April rain and snow event resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration for flooding on April 16, 2023. Initial flood warnings were issued in mid April with peak stages realized in late April. The highest above flood stage was Lynxville Dam at 5.26 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow pushed the Mississippi River at Trempealeau into flood stage on April 16th. The crest of 651.01 ft above sea level on April 25 was 4.01 ft over flood stage. Lake Road abutting the Mississippi River was closed due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and slow moving low pressure area that tracked along the WI and IL border brought heavy snow to central Wisconsin, moderate to heavy freezing rain and ice accumulations over far southern WI, and 1-2 inches of sleet and some snow accumulation in between. Thousands of power outages were prevalent especially over far southern WI where the heaviest ice accumulations occurred. Widespread school cancellations were enacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet accumulation of 3 to 5 inches. Little to no ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record breaking snowfall for the 2022-2023 winter season was received across all of northwest Wisconsin which was about 200% of normal snowfall. Much of this snowpack remained on the ground into April. Warm temperatures 10 to 20 degrees above normal during the second week of the month led to rapid snowmelt across the region with fast rises on area waterways. This above normal warmth was quickly followed by a late season storm that brought heavy rain and heavy snow to the region and further exacerbated the ongoing flooding. Roads began being impacted on the 12th with water over roads and quickly escalated to washouts, failed culverts and gravel roads stripped over the next few days. Most of the runoff had routed through the area by the 20th with conditions improving over the remainder of the month, although localized impacts to basements, farm fields, etc. continued into early May. Due to the extent of the damage to roads, numerous entities declared states of emergency to assist with the recovery efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures early in April led to rapid snowmelt of a well above normal snowpack across the region. Additionally, heavy rainfall on top of the snowpack led to additional high runoff across the county as well. Roads began to be impacted on the 12th with water overtopping low spots leading to washouts, failed culverts and numerous closed roads. Temperatures cooled during the middle of the month along with a heavy rain/snow event the 16th into the 17th. This allowed the runoff to slow and water levels to drop.  Clam Lake rose out of its bank and flooded around 60 homes and cabins around the lake for a time around the 16th and 17th. Multiple communities along with the county issued disaster declarations due to the extent of washed out roads with the worst of the flooding abating by the 25th. Damage estimates were not readily available from the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm at the end of March provided over 1 of rainfall in central to northern Wisconsin on a deeper than average snowpack. The rapid runoff resulted in sites along the Black River in central Wisconsin to quickly rise to flood stage for the beginning of April. Minor to moderate flooding was realized at most sites with the highest height recorded at Black River Falls, with 4.17 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm at the end of March resulted in over an inch of rain falling on a deeper than average snowpack in north-central Wisconsin. The Black River near Hatfield quickly rose above flood stage and crested at 798.83 ft above sea level on April 1st, 1.83 ft above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive storm system affected the Upper Midwest from the evening of February 26th through much of the 27th. This brought a mix of rain, snow, and freezing rain to north-central Wisconsin. Freezing rain accumulations of three tenths of an inch were reported in Dorchester (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported around three tenths of an inch of ice accumulation occurred in Dorchester from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system impacted Wisconsin from the evening of Saturday, April 15th into Sunday night, April 16th. Initially, rain and thunderstorms spread through southern Wisconsin before switching over to snow during the morning hours of Sunday, April 16th. Heaviest snowfall occurred in central Wisconsin during the day Sunday, April 16th, where snowfall totals were 22.5 near Longwood (Clark County) and 22.0 near Black River Falls and Northfield (Jackson County). Many trees were beginning to leaf out following record breaking warmth the week prior, and the heavy snow load resulted in tree damage and power outages. The large amount of precipitation associated with this storm greatly impacted flooding along the Mississippi, Black, and Yellow Rivers and ultimately resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration on April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer public observers reported 8 to 12 inches of snowfall across Monroe County. The highest reported total of 16 inches was recorded in Sparta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm affected much of Wisconsin in the middle of April, providing upwards of 20 of snow in north-central Wisconsin on an already deeper than average snowpack. Subsequent anomalously warm temperatures resulted in a rapid melt of this deep snowpack and rapid rises along the Black River in Central Wisconsin. The highest above flood stage occurred at Black River Falls reaching 6.42 ft above flood stage on April 21st. The combination of this strong spring storm and subsequent flooding impacts resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin declaring a State of Emergency on April 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm in the middle of April provided over 20 of snow on a deeper than average snowpack in north-central Wisconsin, quickly followed by anomalously warm temperatures and rapid melting. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intermittent light snow from Saturday November 25th through Sunday November 26th resulted in slippery road conditions for the busy Thanksgiving holiday travel weekend. Broadcast media and law enforcement reported multiple slide offs in western and central Wisconsin. Through most of the event, snowfall rates remained under 0.1 inches per hour across western Wisconsin, increasing near 0.25 inch per hour on the evening of the 26th. The long duration resulted in a widespread 1 to 2 inches of overall accumulations by Sunday night. Highest snowfall reports were 3.3 inches near Cuba City (Grant County), and 2.0 inches near Cassville (Grant County), Thorp (Clark County), De Soto (Crawford County), and Eastman (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported 1.5 inches of snowfall near White Creek and 1.8 inches of snowfall in Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeper than average snow pack from northern Minnesota into northern Wisconsin underwent a rapid melt through much of April from unseasonably warm temperatures combined with multiple rain events. This resulted in increased water levels and significant flooding for the entire length of the local Mississippi River, with most sites climbing to their third to fifth highest crests on record. A significant mid-April rain and snow event resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration for flooding on April 16, 2023. Initial flood warnings were issued in mid April with peak stages realized in late April. The highest above flood stage was Lynxville Dam at 5.26 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow pushed the Mississippi River at Alma into flood stage from April 22nd to April 27th. The crest of 17.05 ft on April 25 was 1.05 ft over flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter storm brought two rounds of snowfall to northeast Wisconsin from Tuesday evening (2/21) through Thursday morning (2/23).  The first round of snow brought a swath of 2 to 4 inches across the region  from Tuesday evening (2/21) through Wednesday morning (2/22).  The second round of snow brought a swath of 6 to 12 inches from Wednesday afternoon (2/22) through Thursday morning (2/23).  In addition to the snow, strong winds of 30 to 50 mph were reported during the second round of snow, causing considerable blowing and drifting of the snow.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 12 inches near Peshtigo (Marinette Co.), 11.4 inches in Cecil (Shawano Co.), 9.6 inches in Oconto Falls (Oconto Co.), 8.5 inches in Appleton (Outagamie Co.), 8 inches in Omro (Winnebago Co.), Spread Eagle (Florence Co.), as well as Sister Bay (Door Co.), and 7.5 inches in Pulaski (Oconto Co.), Mountain (Oconto Co.), as well as Mosinee (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total in Door County was 8 inches in Sister Bay. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph at times Wednesday night (2/22), causing blowing and drifting snow. The highest recorded wind speed in Door County was 40 mph at Chambers Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm at the end of March resulted in over an inch of rain falling on a deeper than average snowpack in central and northern Wisconsin. The Yellow River near Necedah slowly rose over the following days and crested at 16.32 ft during the evening of April 3rd, which is 1.32 ft above flood stage. Two subsequent crests on April 7th and April 8th were 0.09 ft and 0.4 ft above flood stage, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yellow River near Necedah crested at 16.32 ft  on April 3rd, which is 1.32 ft above flood stage. Two subsequent crests followed over the next few days, both less than 0.5 ft above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system impacted Wisconsin from the evening of Saturday, April 15th into Sunday night, April 16th. Initially, rain and thunderstorms spread through southern Wisconsin before switching over to snow during the morning hours of Sunday, April 16th. Heaviest snowfall occurred in central Wisconsin during the day Sunday, April 16th, where snowfall totals were 22.5 near Longwood (Clark County) and 22.0 near Black River Falls and Northfield (Jackson County). Many trees were beginning to leaf out following record breaking warmth the week prior, and the heavy snow load resulted in tree damage and power outages. The large amount of precipitation associated with this storm greatly impacted flooding along the Mississippi, Black, and Yellow Rivers and ultimately resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration on April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer public observers reported 6 to 8 inches of snowfall across Vernon County. The highest reported total of 10.5 inches was recorded near Westby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall set up across parts of the Northland during the overnight hours of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. Dry air near the surface initially limited snowfall but snow became heavier later in the night and began accumulating. Lake-enhancement was also seen with the highest totals occurring along along the South Shore of Lake Superior. Snowfall in this area was in the 5 to 8 inch range with higher amounts to 10 inches along the terrain ridge in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 8 inches were recorded across Ashland County with a brief break during the evening of the 22nd into the early morning hours of the 23rd before a stronger snow storm started to move into the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought accumulating snow and blizzard conditions to much of northwest Wisconsin starting the evening of the 22nd and continuing into the 23rd. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches were common across the region with higher amounts to around a foot in parts of Sawyer and Price counties. Additionally, strong winds produced blizzard conditions for nearly 12 hours in the Ashland area which resulted in a closure of US 2 west of Ashland due to very low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were reported across the county with a high report of 9.2 inches in Park Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced several days of snow across western Wisconsin. The snow began during the afternoon of February 21st and continued on and off into the morning of the 23rd. Initial light snow accumulations area wide became heavier during the night of the 22nd. The highest overall snowfall totals ranged from 12 to 14 inches from Buffalo into Taylor Counties from two separate bands, an initial band during the evening of the 21st and another heavier band through much of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. The Governor of Wisconsin declared an energy emergency that would allow for the swift and efficient restoration of any electric power outages. Interstate 94 near Osseo (Trempealeau County) was closed for a period during the morning of the 22nd because of a weather related accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that a total of 10 to 13 inches of snow fell across Juneau County. An initial band of snow on the 21st provided 2 to 4 inches with an additional 8 to 10 inches falling from the 22nd into the 23rd. The highest reported total was 13 inches in New Lisbon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few creeks and rivers reached minor flood stage over southeast WI after 1.5-2.0 inches of rain fell on a melting, shallow snowpack and frozen ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Root River and Oak Creek Parkways and adjacent roads and lowland flooded. A few cars were stranded in West Allis with water up to the top of the tires.

Floodwaters surrounded a home at the intersection of Highway 50 and Highway W in the Town of Wheatland. Water was about 8 inches deep over 77th Street in the Town of Wheatland area. Water is in the backyards of some homes in the Village of Salem Lakes and Town of Wheatland areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought accumulating snow and blizzard conditions to much of northwest Wisconsin starting the evening of the 22nd and continuing into the 23rd. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches were common across the region with higher amounts to around a foot in parts of Sawyer and Price counties. Additionally, strong winds produced blizzard conditions for nearly 12 hours in the Ashland area which resulted in a closure of US 2 west of Ashland due to very low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across the county with a high report of 11.3 inches at Gile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced several days of snow across western Wisconsin. The snow began during the afternoon of February 21st and continued on and off into the morning of the 23rd. Initial light snow accumulations area wide became heavier during the night of the 22nd. The highest overall snowfall totals ranged from 12 to 14 inches from Buffalo into Taylor Counties from two separate bands, an initial band during the evening of the 21st and another heavier band through much of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. The Governor of Wisconsin declared an energy emergency that would allow for the swift and efficient restoration of any electric power outages. Interstate 94 near Osseo (Trempealeau County) was closed for a period during the morning of the 22nd because of a weather related accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that a total of 10 to 12 inches of snow fell across Jackson County. An initial band of snow on the 21st provided 2 to 4 inches with an additional 7 to 8 inches falling from the night of the 22nd into the 23rd. The highest reported total was 12 inches near Pray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system impacted Wisconsin from the evening of Saturday, April 15th into Sunday night, April 16th. Initially, rain and thunderstorms spread through southern Wisconsin before switching over to snow during the morning hours of Sunday, April 16th. Heaviest snowfall occurred in central Wisconsin during the day Sunday, April 16th, where snowfall totals were 22.5 near Longwood (Clark County) and 22.0 near Black River Falls and Northfield (Jackson County). Many trees were beginning to leaf out following record breaking warmth the week prior, and the heavy snow load resulted in tree damage and power outages. The large amount of precipitation associated with this storm greatly impacted flooding along the Mississippi, Black, and Yellow Rivers and ultimately resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration on April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer public observers reported 8 to 12 inches of snowfall across Trempealeau County. The highest reported total of 17.0 inches was recorded near Ettrick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm in the middle of April provided over 20 of snow on a deeper than average snowpack in central to northern Wisconsin, quickly followed by anomalously warm temperatures and rapid melting. The Yellow River at Necedah slowly rose and crested at 15.93  ft during the afternoon of April 22nd, which is 0.93 ft above flood stage. Lesser crests of 15.14 ft, 15.52 ft, and 15.66 ft were recorded on April 21st, April 23rd, and April 24th, respectively. The combination of this strong spring storm and subsequent flooding impacts resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin declaring of State of Emergency on April 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yellow River at Necedah crested at 15.93 ft on April 22nd, which is 0.93 ft above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeper than average snow pack from northern Minnesota into northern Wisconsin underwent a rapid melt through much of April from unseasonably warm temperatures combined with multiple rain events. This resulted in increased water levels and significant flooding for the entire length of the local Mississippi River, with most sites climbing to their third to fifth highest crests on record. A significant mid-April rain and snow event resulted in the Governor of Wisconsin issuing a State of Emergency declaration for flooding on April 16, 2023. Initial flood warnings were issued in mid April with peak stages realized in late April. The highest above flood stage was Lynxville Dam at 5.26 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow pushed the Mississippi River at Genoa into flood stage on April 17th. The crest of 635.72 ft above sea level on April 27 was 4.72 ft over flood stage. Water flowed through Genoa Park and into Water Street. Lock & Dam 8 in Genoa was closed, as was Blackhawk Island a few miles downstream in De Soto. The increased flow also resulted in multiple train cars being carried downstream during a train derailment near De Soto on April 27th. Flooded roadside ditches made further recovery efforts into May more challenging.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked along the Wisconsin and Illinois border on Monday (2/27). A surge of warm air aloft led to freezing rain across central and east-central Wisconsin with snow across north-central and northeast Wisconsin. Ice accumulations ranged from one tenth to one third of an inch across central and east-central Wisconsin.  Snowfall accumulations were generally 6 to 9 inches across north-central and northeast Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 9.4 inches in Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), 9 inches in Oconto Falls (Oconto Co.), 8.8 inches near Cecil (Shawano Co.), 8.5 inches in Pulaski (Oconto Co.), 8 inches in Phelps (Vilas Co.), near Shawano (Shawano Co.),  as well as Suring (Oconto Co.).||Some of the highest ice accumulations were 0.35 inches in Marshfield (Wood Co.), 0.28 inches in Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.), and 0.26 inches in Wausau (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest icing totals across Wood County included 0.35 inches in Marshfield and 0.28 inches in Wisconsin Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A red flag warning was in effect over southern Wisconsin on April 12 due to critical fire weather conditions. The Governor of Wisconsin issued Executive Order #191, declaring a state of emergency in response to the elevated wildfire conditions. Multiple wildfires were reported from April 12th to 14th, with the two most notable burning west of Warrens (Monroe County) and near Necedah (Juneau County). These fires burned 3,092 and 100 acres, respectively, with both requiring evacuations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire began in the Town of Necedah on April 12th, spreading towards multiple housing structures. Evacuations of three separate streets in town were implemented. A total of 100 acres burned.

The crest of 15.89 ft on April 26 was 3.89 ft over flood stage. French Island, which abuts La Crosse, had a voluntary evacuation order in place. People were forced to pump water from their basements around the clock and used canoes in the streets.

A surge of warm air aloft led to freezing rain across central and east-central Wisconsin with snow across north-central and northeast Wisconsin. Ice accumulations ranged from one tenth to one third of an inch across central and east-central Wisconsin.  Snowfall accumulations were generally 6 to 9 inches across north-central and northeast Wisconsin.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 9.4 inches in Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), 9 inches in Oconto Falls (Oconto Co.), 8.8 inches near Cecil (Shawano Co.), 8.5 inches in Pulaski (Oconto Co.), 8 inches in Phelps (Vilas Co.), near Shawano (Shawano Co.),  as well as Suring (Oconto Co.).||Some of the highest ice accumulations were 0.35 inches in Marshfield (Wood Co.), 0.28 inches in Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.), and 0.26 inches in Wausau (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total in southern Marinette County was 8 inches in Crivitz as well as Peshtigo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A red flag warning was in effect over southern Wisconsin on April 12 due to critical fire weather conditions. The Governor of Wisconsin issued Executive Order #191, declaring a state of emergency in response to the elevated wildfire conditions. Multiple wildfires were reported from April 12th to 14th, with the two most notable burning west of Warrens (Monroe County) and near Necedah (Juneau County). These fires burned 3,092 and 100 acres, respectively, with both requiring evacuations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Critical fire weather conditions led to a prescribed burn west of Warrens becoming uncontained, and eventually burning 3,092 acres from April 12th to April 14th. Many local roads along Arcadia Avenue were closed due to the fire, as was Interstate 94 and US Highway 12 between Tomah and Black River Falls due to smoke and poor visibility. Many families were evacuated. Three structures were damaged and one shed was destroyed by the flames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm passed through the Mid-Mississippi River Valley during much of the day on February 9th. Tight snowfall bands grazed extreme southwest Wisconsin into the evening hours, with amounts of a trace to 7 inches from northern to southern Grant County, respectively. Highest snowfall accumulation was seen in Kieler (Grant County) with 9.0 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 7 inches of snow fell in far southern areas of Grant County. The highest reported total was 9.0 inches in Kieler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm passed through the Mid-Mississippi River Valley during the day and into the evening of February 16th. Increased, tight snowfall bands grazed southern Wisconsin, with amounts of 1 to 5 inches accumulating from northern to southern Grant County, respectively. The highest snowfall accumulation was seen near Kieler (Grant County) with 7.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 5 inches of snow fell in far southern areas of Grant County. The highest reported total was 7.0 inches near Kieler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought widespread snow across the region. Most locations generally saw 3 to 5 inches, but a few locations picked up over 6 inches in a 24 hour period. Impacts were minimal since much of the snow fell during the day and the treated roads remained wet. This was not a winter storm warning event, but we are including it in storm data since we did have a few counties in a Winter Storm Warning. ||A few noteworthy snow amounts are:|7.5 inches near Altoona (in 24 hours).|6.6 inches just south of Eau Claire.|6.0 inches near Eau Claire.|5.0 inches near Chippewa Falls.|4.5 inches northwest of Pepin.|4.3 inches near Menomonie.|4.0 inches near Ellsworth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple measurements of 3 to 4 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late spring system produced a band of very heavy snow across the region on the evening of March 31, and ending during the early morning of April 01  (see corresponding entry for April). Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour combined with northwest winds of 25 to near 35 mph hour. This was a heavy, wet snow, and as a result there were power outages with the highest snowfall amounts across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin. In some areas, recovery lasted multiple days before full power was restored.||A few notable snowfall amounts are:|15.0 inches near Ladysmith in Rusk County.|13.0 inches near New Richmond in St. Croix County.|11.5 inches near Amery in Polk County.|11.0 inches near Chetek in Barron County.|10.0 inches near Boyceville in Dunn County.|8.0 inches near Ellsworth in Pierce County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix with a couple hours of very heavy snow and strong northwest winds produced hazardous driving conditions. Based on surrounding reports, radar, ASOS observations, and WIDOT road conditions, this event verified at 0100 CST on April 01, before the snow ended during the early morning hours of April 01. A Co-op observer measured 11.5 inches near Amery. In addition to the heavy snow, gusty winds near 30 mph led to periods of whiteout conditions and snow covered roads, which verified the winter storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system that brought strong to severe thunderstorms to parts of western and central Wisconsin during the late afternoon of Friday, March 31, 2023 brought a band of heavy snowfall from late in the evening into the early morning hours of April 1, 2023. Snowfall amounts of 10.0 inches were reported in Taylor County at Rib Lake, Gilman, and near Hannibal. The highest total snowfall was 15.0 inches reported in Westboro (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that 10.0 inches of snow fell in Rib Lake, Gilman, and near Hannibal from the evening of March 31st, 2023 into the morning of April 1st, 2023. The highest observed snowfall was 15.0 inches in Westboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow fall from the southwest metro up through north central Wisconsin. This was driven primarily by frontogenesis. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour at times. A Winter Weather Advisory was in effect for much of the area. A few counties ended up getting 6 to 7 inches of snow in less than 12 hours across northcentral Wisconsin.||A few noteworthy snowfall totals are:|7.1 inches near Shell Lake.|6.8 inches southwest of Shell Lake.|6.7 inches near Benoit Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were no observed snow reports in Polk County, but the bordering counties received 6 to 7 inches of snow near the county line. Radar confirmed that northern Polk county had similar amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of warm-air-advection heavy snow was forecast to move across the region from west to east on the 11th and produce a few inches of snow. Then a second area of snow driven primarily by a 500mb shortwave trough was expected to set up north of the forecast area and lead to a longer duration of snow through the 12th.||The second round of snow persisted a bit longer, and was a bit farther south than forecast. This ended up producing snowfall totals near the higher end of guidance.|Northern part of counties had 10 to 12 inches. 4 to 6 inches was common in Barron County WI and therefore not included in the winter storm. Here are a few notable reports:||Several reports over 9 inches in Ladysmith Wisconsin.|10 inches near Balsam Lake WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snowfall reports of 8 to 10 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from southeastern Colorado into the Upper Mississippi Valley April 4th into April 5th and brought a wintry mix of rain, snow and freezing rain to much of the Northland. Freezing rain was most impactful with this system, especially across areas of northwest Wisconsin adjacent to Lake Superior where up to 0.30 inches of ice accumulated. Northeastern Minnesota saw more of a mix of snow and freezing rain with the Brainerd Lakes area receiving upwards of a quarter of an inch of ice along with a few inches of snow. Areas along the North Shore of Lake Superior received more in the way of snow with just over 6 inches received in areas along the terrain ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain led to accumulations as high as 0.3 across the county with the high report coming from near Solon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure over Wisconsin brought a prolonged period of heavy, wet snow to parts of northwest Wisconsin the 29th into the 30th. Iron and Ashland counties received the brunt of the snowfall with Lake Superior enhancing totals as well. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were received from this area with upwards of 10 inches in Gile. While some snow did fall elsewhere, totals were suppressed by a period of rainfall along with the higher sun angle of late April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought heavy late season snow to the area on the 29th into the 30th. While much of the snow was lost to melting with higher sun angles and rain falling on the snow at times, parts of the county did report snowfall of 6 to 8 inches with the Gile area receiving around 10.5 inches by the time the system ended.

A Co-op observer measured 11.0 inches Chetek. In addition to the heavy snow, gusty winds near 30 mph led to periods of whiteout conditions and snow covered roads, which verified the winter storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system strengthened across Wisconsin and produced a band of very heavy snow in eastern Rusk, Chippewa, and Eau Claire counties. Snowfall ranged from around 2-4 inches in the western part of the county, to near 12 inches in the eastern part of the county. Farther east in the neighboring counties in the ARX forecast area, a few locations had over 20 inches.||A few noteworthy reports are:|12 inches 6 miles east of Chippewa Falls in Chippewa County.|10 inches near Big Falls in Rusk County.|6 inches 5 miles SSE of Eau Claire in Eau Claire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary deformation band over Central Wisconsin provided a well-defined gradient of heavy snowfall. The highest snowfall totals were confined to the eastern region of Rusk County. COOP observers measured 10 inches of snowfall at the Big Falls Hydroelectric Station or 10 miles northeast of Ladysmith, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gravity waves due to ducting generated a narrow line of strong wind across Central Minnesota to Eastern Minnesota on the morning of April 19th, 2023. Early that morning a weak system produced precipitation across the region. Upper-Air soundings from NWS Twin Cities detailed a low-level inversion/stable layer at approximately 6,000 feet above the surface with remnant instability from the previous rainfall early that morning. Over the Central Plains, low pressure was developing an associated warm front that extended over Iowa was advancing towards Minnesota. Meanwhile in the upper-levels, a trough was beginning to dig over the Western Dakotas. As these features progress northeastward, remnant low-level forcing would interact with the capped layer and be refracted back towards the ground and creating a line of gusty winds across the surface. ||A report of strong wind occurred in Polk County, St. Croix Falls, WI where a farm shed was overturned onto its side. The ASOS station located at the L.O. Simenstad Municipal Airport in Osceola, WI recorded a 45 mph gust just before 9 AM CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk County emergency management forwarded a photo a damaged farm shed in St. Croix Falls, WI as a result of strong winds. It was also reported that a few shingles were blown off of the same residence as to where the shed damaged occurred. Sustained winds at 40 mph with gusts 45 mph gusts were measured at the ASOS observation site located at the L.O. Simenstad Municipal Airport in Osceola, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, deepening area of low pressure located over the Upper Peninsula of Michigan affected the Northland on the 16th into the 17th bringing a period of freezing rain before changing over to snow. Areas closer to the low in northwest Wisconsin received the brunt of the snow with upwards of 10 to 15 inches in areas near Lake Superior where lake enhancement occurred. As the low deepened the night of the 16th into the 17th, winds increased and became quite breezy along the South Shore leading to a period of blizzard conditions the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of snow was observed across the eastern part of the county on the 16th into the 17th. Snowfall totals were highest in the northeastern corner of the county where totals of 7 to 12 inches were received. A high report of 13 inches was reported in Waino.

The WI River at Portage was in flood much of the month of April from the combination of snowmelt and precipitation in the middle to upper basins of the river. The river reached moderate flood stage and was just short of reaching major flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding of the Blackhawk Park and Long Lake areas occurred with many flooded roads and road closures affecting about 50 people. Some seasonal homes along Old River Road were surrounded by floodwaters. Levee Rd., west of Portage but south of the river, was partially flooded. On the west side of Portage and north of the river, floodwaters affected River Shores Rd.  Farther south in the Town of Dekorra, about a dozen homes and some outbuildings are flooded near Highway V.  Floodwaters flowed over portions of County Highways V, J, River Oaks Road, and Oak Knoll Road. Some residents had to use canoes or boats for transportation to and from their homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Forest County included 18.8 inches near Argonne, 18 inches in Armstrong Creek, and 11 inches near Crandon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record breaking snowfall for the 2022-2023 winter season was received across all of northwest Wisconsin which was about 200% of normal snowfall. Much of this snowpack remained on the ground into April. Warm temperatures 10 to 20 degrees above normal during the second week of the month led to rapid snowmelt across the region with fast rises on area waterways. This above normal warmth was quickly followed by a late season storm that brought heavy rain and heavy snow to the region and further exacerbated the ongoing flooding. Roads began being impacted on the 12th with water over roads and quickly escalated to washouts, failed culverts and gravel roads stripped over the next few days. Most of the runoff had routed through the area by the 20th with conditions improving over the remainder of the month, although localized impacts to basements, farm fields, etc. continued into early May. Due to the extent of the damage to roads, numerous entities declared states of emergency to assist with the recovery efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures early in April led to rapid snowmelt of a well above normal snowpack across the region. Additionally, heavy rainfall on top of the snowpack led to additional high runoff across the county as well. Roads began to be impacted on the 11th with water overtopping low spots leading to washouts and failed culverts. Temperatures cooled during the middle of the month along with a heavy snow event the 16th into the 17th. This allowed the runoff to slow and water levels to drop.  Numerous road closures were reported across the entire county due to failed culverts, washouts and gravel roads being washed away. Multiple communities issued disaster declarations due to the extent of washed out roads. Copper Falls State Park was also closed for a period due to damaged roads within the park. Damage estimates were not readily available from the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure advanced across the Central Region of the United States. Given its track, the northern sector of this storm contained plentiful moisture and warm air advection from the south. Mixed precipitation types were observed with this storm. For Western Wisconsin, most areas would observe a mix of rain and snow in the overnight hours Sunday night and a transition to freezing rain during the daytime hours Monday. A winter storm warning was issued for potential of 2 inches of snowfall, up to 1/4 inch of ice accumulation, and 40 mph wind gusts. ||Only a few counties would observe ice significant ice accumulation. Chippewa Valley Regional Airport observed 0.28 inches of ice, and an observer near River Falls, WI reported 0.25 inches.  In St. Croix County, 16 calls of assistance were received due to vehicles off of the roadway from icy road conditions. Snowfall was observed in parts of the region however accumulations were null to a tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers freezing rain accumulations across the county. A tenth of an inch of ice accumulation was recorded about 6 miles south of Ellsworth while a quarter of an inch of ice was reported near River Falls, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in moderate to major flooding stages being reached at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Wisconsin. Initial minor flood stage was reached in mid April with near record crests near the end of April. The flooding impacts resulted in a state of emergency being issued by the Governor of Wisconsin. Drier conditions during the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites in Wisconsin in early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the fourth highest crest on record at the Lynxville Dam towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage at the Lynxville Dam on May 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Developing low pressure tracking across the Ohio River Valley, spread a swath of moderate to heavy wet snow across southern WI. Lake enhancement brought the highest snow totals to the counties adjacent to Lake MI. The wet and heavy snow caused tree branches to fall onto power lines with power outages affecting 100,000 customers, mainly in the lake counties. Numerous schools chose to do a 2 hour delay or close. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred on slick roads across southern WI, which included one fatality in Fond du Lac County when a vehicle slammed into a jackknifed semi-truck on I-41 at highway N shortly after 4 AM CST on March 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 6-8 inches. One fatality occurred when a SUV slammed into a jackknifed semi-truck on slippery roads on I-41 north of Fond du Lac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across southern Wisconsin on the evening of March 31st which had numerous thunderstorms develop along it.  Most of the stronger storms that interacted with the warm front ended up producing some brief EF-0 to EF-1 tornadic damage.  There were a few incidents that looked to be from straight line winds along the southeast side of the rotations, but most of the damage appeared to be lofted and from tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of farm buildings had substantial damage to them, particularly a large farm operation along County Road G where an empty grain bin was destroyed and a large shed lost it's I beam and the roof collapsed inward.  A row of 13 power poles were snapped due to the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system brought a swath of heavy snow to parts of northwest Wisconsin on the 1st. A band of 3 to 7 inches of accumulation stretched across Douglas, Bayfield and Ashland counties and into parts of Washburn County as well. Lake-enhancement led to a high total of 8 inches in the city of Washburn in Bayfield County. Strong winds on the backside of the system led to blowing and drifting snow into the 2nd before high pressure built in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were reported across the county with a high report of 7.1 inches near Superior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Developing low pressure tracking across the Ohio River Valley, spread a swath of moderate to heavy wet snow across southern WI. Lake enhancement brought the highest snow totals to the counties adjacent to Lake MI. The wet and heavy snow caused tree branches to fall onto power lines with power outages affecting 100,000 customers, mainly in the lake counties. Numerous schools chose to do a 2 hour delay or close. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred on slick roads across southern WI, which included one fatality in Fond du Lac County when a vehicle slammed into a jackknifed semi-truck on I-41 at highway N shortly after 4 AM CST on March 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 4-6 inches but much less on the roads due to relatively mild air and road temperatures.

Heavier bands of snowfall setup across the region leading to enhanced snow amounts across northwestern Wisconsin along with some additional shower activity later in the afternoon before the system completely cleared the area. Snowfall totals of 4 to 7 inches were reported under these heavier bands with higher amounts to around 8 inches from Spooner into the Hayward area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 6 inches were recorded across the county with a high report of 7 inches in South Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in moderate to major flooding stages being reached at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Wisconsin. Initial minor flood stage was reached in mid April with near record crests near the end of April. The flooding impacts resulted in a state of emergency being issued by the Governor of Wisconsin. Drier conditions during the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites in Wisconsin in early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the fourth highest crest on record at the Trempealeau Dam towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage at Trempealeau Dam on May 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved to the south and east of the region on the 16th and into the 17th leading to snowfall across the Northland. The heaviest amounts were near Lake Superior where 3 to 10 inches were common with higher amounts to around 14 inches along parts of the Bayfield Peninsula with lake and terrain enhancement. Snowfall was heavy and wet for this event and, when combined with the well above normal snowfall received already this season, a section of the roof at a school in Solon Springs began to sag and led to students not returning to school following spring break for an additional week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 6 inches were reported along the terrain ridge over north-central Douglas County with a high report of 10 inches near Hawthorne. Snow build-up on the roof of a school in Solon Springs led to sagging trusses on the 16th and the school was closed for an additional week following spring break as crews worked to remove the snow and repair the roof.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall varied greatly across the county with under 3 inches in the southern part of the county and in areas adjacent to Lake Superior. Locations along the higher terrain extending southwest from the Bayfield Peninsula received 6 to 12 inches of snow with a high amount of 14.1 inches near Bayfield.

Lake enhancement brought the highest snow totals to the counties adjacent to Lake MI. The wet and heavy snow caused tree branches to fall onto power lines with power outages affecting 100,000 customers, mainly in the lake counties. Numerous schools chose to do a 2 hour delay or close. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred on slick roads across southern WI, which included one fatality in Fond du Lac County when a vehicle slammed into a jackknifed semi-truck on I-41 at highway N shortly after 4 AM CST on March 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 6-9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure lifted northeast across the Great Lakes on Saturday (3/25), producing a narrow 30-mile wide band of heavy snow across eastern Wisconsin.  This heavy band of snow remained nearly stationary, producing 2 to 5 inch per hour snowfall rates with 10 to 20 inches of snow over the Fox Valley northeast towards Door County.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 21.5 inches near Kimberly (Outagamie Co.), 20 inches in Menasha (Winnebago Co.), 18 inches near Combined Locks (Calumet Co.) as well as Neenah (Winnebago Co.), 17 inches in Appleton (Outagamie Co.), 16 inches in Green Bay (Brown Co.), near Darboy (Calumet Co.), and Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.), 10.5 inches near Sister Bay (Door Co.), and 10 inches near Dyckesville (Kewaunee Co.) as well as in Fish Creek (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall total in Kewaunee County was 10 inches near Dyckesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure over eastern Lake Superior led to a prolonged period of lake-effect snowfall across northern Iron County on the 28th into the 29th. Most areas received around 3 to 5 inches while Gile received 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lake-effect snow band produced a swath of 3 to 5 inches of snowfall over the northern part of the county with a high report of 6 inches in Gile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from near Omaha the morning of the 31st to central Lake Michigan by the morning of the 1st. While most of the effects from this system remained to the south of the Northland, a few of our northwest Wisconsin counties did received snowfall on the order of 2 to 8 inches. Price County, being closer to the low, did pile up 15 inches of snow near Phillips during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported across the county with a high report of 10.5 inches near Sarona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in moderate to major flooding stages being reached at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Wisconsin. Initial minor flood stage was reached in mid April with near record crests near the end of April. The flooding impacts resulted in a state of emergency being issued by the Governor of Wisconsin. Drier conditions during the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites in Wisconsin in early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the third highest crest and major flooding in La Crosse towards the end of April. Reports included structure flooding on abutting French Island, closures of many downtown parks, and water over roadways. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage at the La Crosse Dam on May 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system dumped heavy snow on portions of western Wisconsin from March 9th into the 10th. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 4 to 8 inch range. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Muscoda (Grant County), near Blue River (Grant County) and in Lyndon Station (Juneau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across Adams County. The highest reported total was 7.5 inches east of Big Flats.

Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions remained constant across southwest and central Wisconsin through the month of November from long-standing abnormally dry conditions. While the previous month of October provided slightly above normal rainfall amounts, total rainfall near 20 inches from April 1 to October 31st was 5 to 15 inches below normal. During November, only 1 to 3 days with measurable precipitation provided 0.1 to 0.5 inches of total rainfall, near 2 inches below the monthly normal. While the growing season was all but over, multiple days of above normal temperatures, 10 to 20 degrees above normal, inhibited the ground from freezing. Drought conditions remained unchanged through November, with severe drought (D2) encompassing much of Crawford, Richland, and Vernon counties, as well as southern Monroe, Juneau, and Adams counties and northern Grant County. A small area of extreme drought (D3) affected northwest Grant and southern Crawford counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted for the southeastern periphery of Monroe County through November. This was a result of monthly precipitation totals near 2 inches below normal and anomalously warm temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon of July 24, 2023 and produced isolated large hail and localized downburst winds across western Wisconsin. The largest hail occurred in St Croix County, where golf ball size hail was observed near Hudson, along with wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many ten to twelve inch diameter trees blown down.  Corn was also stripped from hail.

Drought conditions spread across the Northland during the month of June with all areas seeing at least D0 Abnormally dry conditions by the end of the month. Some pockets of D2 Severe Drought began to appear during the middle of the month around the head of Lake Superior. Rainfall for the month of June was generally around 1 to 3 inches below normal aside from some parts of the Arrowhead that received above normal rainfall. Most areas on received around 50 percent of normal rainfall for the month leading to stress on crops and area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following record snowfall over the preceding winter, drought conditions quickly spread across the Northland during the spring. A small area of D2 Significant Drought was added to northwestern Douglas County with the update on the 20th and persisted through the remainder of the month. Additional expansion across much of the remainder of the northern part of the county was added with the update on the 27th. Rainfall deficits in excess of 2.5 inches were observed across much of Douglas County which is about 40 to 60 percent of normal for the month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions spread across the Northland during the month of June with all areas seeing at least D0 Abnormally dry conditions by the end of the month. Some pockets of D2 Severe Drought began to appear during the middle of the month around the head of Lake Superior. Rainfall for the month of June was generally around 1 to 3 inches below normal aside from some parts of the Arrowhead that received above normal rainfall. Most areas on received around 50 percent of normal rainfall for the month leading to stress on crops and area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following record snowfall over the preceding winter, drought conditions quickly spread across the Northland during the spring. A small area of D2 Significant Drought was added to far northwestern Bayfield County with the update on the 27th and persisted through the remainder of the month. Rainfall deficits in excess of 2.5 inches were observed across much of Bayfield County which is about 30 to 50 percent of normal for the month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed along a weak boundary. There was 1 report of quarter size hail in New Richmond WI.
Daytime instability combined with lake breezes and outflow boundaries to create isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon on Saturday (6/3).  The strongest storm produced hail up to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Antigo.
