EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through on July 29th 2023 kicking waves up to around 3 feet during the afternoon. Winds relaxed to around 1 foot at the time of the incident, but lingering currents were present. Witnesses stated the father and son were struggling to fight the currents, which were likely a combination of rip and longshore currents as winds were northeast during the event. The son was rescued by a nearby jet skier, but the father was unable to hold on and later drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through on July 29th 2023 kicking waves up to around 3 feet during the afternoon. Winds relaxed to around 1 foot at the time of the incident, but lingering currents were still present. Witnesses stated the father and son were struggling to fight the currents, which were likely a combination of rip and longshore currents as winds were northeast during the event. The son was rescued by a nearby jet skier, but the father was unable to hold on and later drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought that developed during the summer continued into the winter. Severe Drought (D2) was confined to the southwest corner of Waushara County, with Moderate Drought (D1) over much of the remainder of the county during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought that developed during the summer continued into the winter across Waushara County. Precipitation totals were closer to normal than compared to November. Severe Drought (D2) was confined to the southwest corner of the county, with Moderate Drought (D1) over the remainder of the county during the month. The precipitation total at Hancock was 1.02 inches, which was 0.38 inches below normal or 72% of normal. From June 1 to December 31, Hancock received 17.66 inches of precipitation, which 3.01 inches below normal or 85% of normal. At Wautoma, the precipitation total was 1.40 inches, which was only 0.28 inches below normal or 83% of normal. From June 1 to December 31, Wautoma received 18.86 inches of precipitation, which was 4.97 inches below normal or 79% of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought that developed during the summer across north-central and far northeast Wisconsin continued into the winter. The most pronounced drought conditions were noted across the southwest corner of Forest, northern Langlade, Lincoln, much of Oneida, and far western Vilas counties where Severe Drought (D2) conditions prevailed through December. The Severe Drought (D2) conditions expanded into portions of northeast Forest, much of Florence, and far northern Marinette Counties in November and continued through December. The remainder of north-central and far northeast Wisconsin remained in Abnormally Dry (D0) or Moderate Drought (D1) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally Dry (D0) to Moderate (D1) conditions developed during the summer and worsened in the late fall and early winter. By late November and through December, Severe Drought (D2) encompassed the eastern two-thirds of Florence County with Moderate Drought (D1) over the remainder of the county. At Florence, the precipitation total during November and December was 1.54 inches, which was 1.57 inches below normal or 50% of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through December resulted in no changes to the severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought in parts of southwest and south-central Wisconsin through December. Total rainfall of 10 to 20 from severe drought (D2) onset in June 2023 to the beginning of December was 4 to 14 below normal. Well above normal temperatures during December, about 10 degrees above normal, perpetuated ongoing soil moisture concerns. Monthly rainfall was near 2 for parts of far southwest Wisconsin and near 1 into west-central Wisconsin. While these amounts were near normal, the ongoing abnormally dry conditions resulted in drought severity remaining unchanged through December. Broadcast media reported stunted growth and financial impacts for Christmas tree farms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted in parts of Adams County through December. While monthly total rainfall of 1 to 2  was only slightly below normal, the long standing deficit of more 8 to 10 resulted in the drought remaining unchanged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of light snow and mist was reported by automated weather observation stations in southwest Wisconsin on the morning of December 5th, 2023. A slide off accident was reported along Vernon County Road V in Hillsboro, WI (Vernon County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of light snow and mist was reported by automated weather observation stations in southwest Wisconsin on the morning of December 5th, 2023. A slide off accident was reported by the Vernon County Sherriff's Office along Vernon County Road V in Hillsboro, WI. The automobile left the roadway while attempting a turn, crashing into a residential garage. The lone vehicle occupant sustained serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across parts of northwestern Wisconsin during the month of December. Areas of D2 Severe Drought covered parts or all of Iron, Ashland, Bayfield, Sawyer and Price counties with no changes in coverage observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for the month ended up near to above normal over most of the area thanks to a warm storm that moved through the region during the Christmas holiday period bringing rainfall to most areas. The rainfall was not enough, however, to make much of a difference in the overall dry conditions. Portions of Iron, Bayfield and Price counties were D2 Severe Drought was in place actually ended up below normal for the month in terms of precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought persisted across the southern three-quarters of the county through the month with no changes in status observed. Precipitation was within an inch of normal both above and below for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial heavy snow band reached far southwest Wisconsin late on December 8th with a subsequent area of light snow lasting through December 9th from western into central Wisconsin. Most places received around 1 of accumulation through the longer duration event. Highest snowfall reports were 2.8 in Viroqua (Vernon County) and 2.7 in Friendship (Adams County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of light snow and rain moved through Trempealeau County late on December 9th.  A single vehicle rollover accident was reported along Wisconsin State Highway 93 with one injury. Nearby automated weather observation stations reported several hours of light snow late into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of northwestern Wisconsin on December 17, 2022, as a quick moving cold front moved across the Northland during the evening hours. A few squalls brought heavy snow and very low visibilities that were very brief but impactful to areas along the US2 and US53 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. A webcam at the intersection of US 53 and US 63 in Trego showed no snow on roads before a brief period of very low visibility followed by snow covered roads indicative of the passage of a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front passed through, with around 1500 to 2000 J/kg of SBCAPE ahead of it. Deep layer shear was 30 to 40 kts. An isolated supercell developed in western Wisconsin and produced 1 to 1.75 inch hail in Pierce County. There was also a tornado warning issued, but no reports of damage or a funnel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two local storm reports from trained spotters reported quarter to an inch and a quarter sized hail between the towns of El Paso, WI and Maiden Rock, WI. Or, approximately 5 miles ESE of Ellsworth, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the Northland during the day on the 19th setting the stage for severe storms during the afternoon and evening across the region. The storms that developed were prolific hail producers with hail to the size of limes reported in Burnett and Washburn counties that led to damage. While hail was the primary threat from these storms, one storm did produce a large area of tree damage near Cable with a few other scattered reports of tree damage in the area as well.

Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions remained constant across southwest and central Wisconsin through the month of November from long-standing abnormally dry conditions. While the previous month of October provided slightly above normal rainfall amounts, total rainfall near 20 inches from April 1 to October 31st was 5 to 15 inches below normal. During November, only 1 to 3 days with measurable precipitation provided 0.1 to 0.5 inches of total rainfall, near 2 inches below the monthly normal. While the growing season was all but over, multiple days of above normal temperatures, 10 to 20 degrees above normal, inhibited the ground from freezing. Drought conditions remained unchanged through November, with severe drought (D2) encompassing much of Crawford, Richland, and Vernon counties, as well as southern Monroe, Juneau, and Adams counties and northern Grant County. A small area of extreme drought (D3) affected northwest Grant and southern Crawford counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted across most of Vernon County through November. This was a result of monthly precipitation totals near 2 inches below normal and anomalously warm temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over northern Manitoba trailed a cold front across the Northland on July 3rd. Storms developed ahead of the front during the afternoon and evening hours with storms becoming strong to severe across northwest Wisconsin. These storms took on a linear mode with an associated damaging wind threat as they moved across the region. These damaging winds led to reports of downed trees and powerlines as they progressed to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a car near Park Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intermittent light snow from Saturday November 25th through Sunday November 26th resulted in slippery road conditions for the busy Thanksgiving holiday travel weekend. Broadcast media and law enforcement reported multiple slide offs in western and central Wisconsin. Through most of the event, snowfall rates remained under 0.1 inches per hour across western Wisconsin, increasing near 0.25 inch per hour on the evening of the 26th. The long duration resulted in a widespread 1 to 2 inches of overall accumulations by Sunday night. Highest snowfall reports were 3.3 inches near Cuba City (Grant County), and 2.0 inches near Cassville (Grant County), Thorp (Clark County), De Soto (Crawford County), and Eastman (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer observer reported 0.7 inches of snowfall near Melrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions rapidly worsened across the Northland during the month of July. After record snowfall in many areas over the winter, dry conditions took hold for the spring into the summer with rainfall deficits of 1 to 3 inches for the month across much of northwestern Wisconsin, although there was an area of slightly above normal rainfall from eastern Bayfield County into southern Ashland, northeastern Sawyer and far northern Price counties.  D1 Moderate Drought quickly took hold across the area during the beginning of the month with D2 Severe Drought spreading across much of the region as the month progressed. An area of D3 Extreme Drought was introduced during the middle of the month from northern Douglas County into northern Bayfield County as the sandy soils continued to dry out. Area creeks and rivers dropped to low flow levels and crops began to suffer across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: July began with D1 Moderate Drought conditions covering the county. An area of D2 Severe Drought spread into the southeastern corner of the county with the update on the 11th. D2 Severe Drought then spread across much of the remainder of the county by the 25th outside of a small sliver in the far southwestern corner. Rainfall for the month was around 2 to 4 inches below normal and was exacerbated by the sandy soil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and humid conditions developed across northeastern Minnesota on the 27th ahead of a frontal boundary that stalled out across the region. Storms were primarily hail producers across northeastern Minnesota but transitioned to more of a wind threat as they grew upscale across northwestern Wisconsin. Numerous reports of trees and powerlines downed were received across the area with some flash flooding occurring later in the afternoon as well in areas that saw multiple rounds of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down across a road. Additionally, widespread tree and powerline damage was reported across the county from the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive Heat occurred for portions of western Wisconsin through the afternoon of August 22nd, 2023. A warm front stalled over the region which allowed for a continued supply of warm moist air into the region. Highs ranged in the 90s to low 100s, dew points in the 70s, with heat indices ranging between 105-120 degrees.||The highest apparent temperatures recorded were:|New Richmond, WI (St. Croix) at 119��F|Osceola, WI (Polk) at 114��F|Hager City, WI (Pierce) at 113��F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm environmental temperatures in the mid 90s combined with high dewpoint values in the mid and upper 70s caused high heat indices exceeding 105��F for over three hours. The AWOS station located in Hager City, WI (in adjacent Pierce County) first recorded its highest apparent temperature of 113��F at 4:15 PM (CST). It remained over 105��F through 8:15 PM (CST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms moved through central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 25th. Two reports of observed funnel clouds were received in Clark County, near Thorp and Neillsville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near Neillsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms through the first half of August in parts of western and central Wisconsin provided one half to 3 inches of rainfall, abating any initial expansion of the ongoing severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3). Through the latter half of August, total rain fall remained at or below 1 inch, which was 1 to 3 inches below normal. This resulted in expansion of the severe and extreme drought areas, affecting approximately 70 percent of the local area. Farmers reported variable crop conditions with overall yields 15 to 30 percent of normal. Well below normal cuttings of hay resulted in farmers having to purchase feed for livestock. As as a result of the inclement growing conditions, the United States Department of Agriculture issued disaster declarations for western Wisconsin. The Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources reported an increase in wildfires attributed to the ongoing drought as 6 times as many acres had already been consumed by wildfires compared to the same time the previous year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 7 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought, worsened to extreme drought (D3) for nearly all of Juneau County through August. Total monthly rainfall near 2 inches was 2 to 3 inches below normal, or 20 to 50% of normal total monthly rainfall. By the end of August, extreme drought (D3)  expanded, impacting 35% of Juneau County or 523 square miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved through western and central Wisconsin during evening of August 11th, resulting in coinciding corridors of severe reports. In western Wisconsin, volunteer observers reported toppled trees and power lines from severe wind gusts from Buffalo to Richland County. Highest wind gusts included an estimated 80 mph from a NWS storm survey of extensive damage in Westby (Vernon County) and a measured gust of 68 mph in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) by an automated weather observation station. In central Wisconsin, volunteer observers reported 60 mph wind gusts and quarter inch hail near Loyal (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Sheriff's Office reported a portable toilet was blown across the road near Alma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall and warmer than average temperatures resulted in severe drought conditions developing in portions of west-central and central Wisconsin towards the end of June. Between April 1st and severe drought development by June 27th, a total of 6 to 8 inches of rain fell in Vernon County. This was 6 to 8 inches below normal, or 25 to 50 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 285 square miles or 31% of Monroe County through June.

Under 1.5 inches of rain fell during the second half of August, up to 2 inches below normal, resulting in at extreme drought (D3) for 52% or 608 square miles of Grant County. The United States Department of Agriculture declared Grant County as a contiguous natural disaster area due to the drought on August 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall and warmer than average temperatures resulted in severe drought conditions developing in southwest Wisconsin in the middle of June. The largest rainfall deficit was recorded at the automated weather observation station in Boscobel (Grant County) at 7.84 inches below the normal of 11.51 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While parts of the 2023 summer had warm to hot temperatures, it took until mid to late August for the combination of hot temperatures and higher humidity levels to build a heat wave. Excessive heat advisories and warnings were initially issued on Tuesday, August 22nd and lasted through Thursday, August 24th. Heat indices reached and exceeded 100 degrees each day, with overall highest heat indices occurring on Wednesday, August 23rd. During this time, maximum daytime heat indices reached 120 degrees in southwest Wisconsin at Fennimore (Grant County), Lancaster (Grant County), and the City of La Crosse (La Crosse County). Many sites from western into central Wisconsin recorded heat indices of 100 to 120 degrees over the three days from August 22nd to August 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions through the summer months persisted through August resulting in severe drought (D2) issued for 75% of Jackson County. Total monthly rainfall or 1 to 2 inches across Jackson County was approximately 3 inches below normal. Most of this rain fell during the first half of August, initially abating any expansion of Wisconsin's widespread severe to extreme drought into Jackson County. The second half of August recorded 0.2 to 1 inch of rainfall, 1 to 2.5 inches below normal for the time period. A volunteer farmer reported widespread drought impacts at the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions resulted in severe drought (D2) issued for 75% of Jackson County at the end of August. County wide total monthly rainfall averaged 1 inch and was approximately 3 inches below average, or approximately half of normal monthly rainfall totals. For the final drought issuance of August, severe drought (D2) was issued for 753 square miles or 75% of Jackson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall and warmer than average temperatures resulted in severe drought conditions developing in portions of west-central and central Wisconsin towards the end of June. Between April 1st and severe drought development by June 27th, a total of 6 to 8 inches of rain fell in Vernon County. This was 6 to 8 inches below normal, or 25 to 50 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 110 square miles or 16% of Adams County through June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms through the first half of August in parts of western and central Wisconsin provided one half to 3 inches of rainfall, abating any initial expansion of the ongoing severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3). Through the latter half of August, total rain fall remained at or below 1 inch, which was 1 to 3 inches below normal. This resulted in expansion of the severe and extreme drought areas, affecting approximately 70 percent of the local area. Farmers reported variable crop conditions with overall yields 15 to 30 percent of normal. Well below normal cuttings of hay resulted in farmers having to purchase feed for livestock. As as a result of the inclement growing conditions, the United States Department of Agriculture issued disaster declarations for western Wisconsin. The Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources reported an increase in wildfires attributed to the ongoing drought as 6 times as many acres had already been consumed by wildfires compared to the same time the previous year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 7 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2), worsened to extreme drought (D3) for the entirety of Crawford County through August. Monthly rainfall total neared 1 inch, which mostly fell in the first half of August. The monthly rainfall total was at least 2.5 inches below normal, or 10 to 50% of normal. Through the latter half of August, extreme drought (D3) expanded through the western half of Crawford County, impacting all 594 square miles of the county. The United States Department of Agriculture declared Crawford County as a contiguous natural disaster area due to the drought on August 17th.

Many sites from western into central Wisconsin recorded heat indices of 100 to 120 degrees over the three days from August 22nd to August 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of anomalously high temperatures and humidity on Tuesday, August 22nd and Wednesday, August 23rd resulted in maximum heat indices of 100 to 120 degrees across Grant County. The maximum heat index reached 106 degrees on Tuesday in Muscoda and 120 degrees on Wednesday at the automated weather observation stations in Fennimore and Lancaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged hot and humid conditions coupled with dry mid-levels in the atmosphere, led to extreme amounts of mixed-layer convective available potential energy (MLCAPE). The existence of deep layer shear allowed for numerous strong updrafts to develop across parts of Western WI with the primary hazards being large hail and damaging winds in the afternoon and early evening hours. ||Hail measurements ranged between nickel (0.88 inch) to tea-cup (3.0 inch) size. The largest measured hail was reported 2.5 miles north of Bloomer or 5 miles NW of Chippewa Falls, WI. ||Strong winds uprooted trees, broke large tree branches across, and brought down powerlines across much of the region affected by these storms. The areas with the most wind damage occurred along a line from Polk to Rusk Counties. Strongest wind gusts were estimated to be at least 50 knots or 57 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds caused numerous tree branches measuring 8-10 inches in diameter to fall 10 miles north of St. Croix Falls, WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that were associated with a southward advancing cold front proceeded across Central and Southern MN and Western WI. The meteorological setup was considered a High CAPE/Low Shear environment. Or in other words, plenty of available energy for storms to develop, but lack of turning winds with height to support rotating updrafts. Therefore, large hail was the primary threat with the line of storms that developed from Cumberland, WI down to Redwood Falls, MN and farther south.||These strong storms produced 50 kt wind gusts which snapped a 10-inch diameter tree 8 miles NNE of Menomonie, WI (Dunn) and large hail. Measured hail sizes from these storms ranged from nickel size to golf ball size hail with most falling as quarter size. A few of the highest hailstones measured: |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 4 miles N of Holcombe, WI (Chippewa)|1.50 inches - Ping Pong Ball - 5 miles E of Hudson, WI (St. Croix)|1.25 inches - Half-Dollar - 7 miles SW of Ladysmith, WI (Rusk).

This was followed by another storm in the afternoon that produced a funnel cloud, with one report of tree damage. A tornado warning was issued, but there were no confirmed tornadoes.

Surface temperatures reaching the mid to upper 90's combined with dew points in the 70s and lower 80s allowed for apparent temperatures, or the heat index, to exceed 105��F in for multiple hours. Strong ridging aloft was in place over the southwestern U.S. which allowed for seasonally warm high temperatures to be carried east via zonal flow over the Central U.S. ||In the lower levels, a surface low over the Dakotas along with transport of gulf moisture from the south allowed for dew point temperatures to remain elevated the night prior to July 27th, despite cloud cover lingering from prior convection over the northern plains. As clouds cleared the next day, daytime heating increased surface temperatures in an already humid air mass, causing heat indices to reach dangerous levels. ||The highest heat indices from this event were:|New Richmond Regional Airport in St. Croix County: 115��F|Osceola Municipal Airport in Polk County: 114��F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm environmental temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with high dewpoint values reaching 80��, caused high heat indices exceeding 105��F for approximately four hours. The ASOS station located at the Osceola Municipal Airport recorded an apparent temperature of 114��F at 2:15 PM (CST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subltle shortwave trough within northwest flow moved across the region and provided enought lift for scattered afternoon thunderstorms. There was sufficient MLCAPE (1500 J/kg) and Effective Shear (30kts) for some stronger updrafts that produced severe hail and damaging wind gusts. Overall these storms were relatively short lived and died off once the outflow undercut the updraft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail coated the ground after just 2 minutes, and ended at 347 PM CDT. Largest hail was golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms developed across the region and produce widespread hail, with occasional strong wind gusts and wind damage. Of particular note was hail that was 2 to 2.5 inches south of Rice Lake WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm caused numerous large tree branches down around the town of Menomonie. Some branches were estimated to be at least 8 inches in diameter. In addition, the Menomonie ASOS measured a 53 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported that the storm caused scattered trees down across nearly a one mile swath from the northwest side of Cornell. Some trees fell on houses and vehicles. A gazebo was also damaged, several garage doors buckled, and some shingles damaged.
